data.title: Hims & Hers Declines - How The GLP-1 Market Shift Affects Growth
data.description:
Hims & Hers Health (NYSE:HIMS) stock plummeted 25% last Friday after the U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA) declared that Ozempic and Wegovy (semaglutide) are no longer in shortage. As a result, state-licensed pharmacies and outsourcing facilities must stop compounding these medications by April 22, 2025, and May 22, 2025, respectively. Through its state-licensed 503A compounding pharmacies and FDA-registered 503B outsourcing facility, Hims offered compounded versions of semaglutide, a popular GLP-1 receptor agonist, during the shortage. Novo Nordisk’s (NVO) semaglutide is a multi-billion-dollar weight loss and diabetes drug that undoubtedly boosted interest in Hims’ weight loss services (20,000 new subscribers from pre-existing customers in Q3 alone). Because weight loss is a chronic disease that requires extended treatment, many of Hims’ subscribers will soon have to obtain their semaglutide from Novo Nordisk. Weight loss is one of the four key services on Hims’ platform (the others are sexual health, dermatology, and mental health) and carries a “higher monthly average price” relative to the other services.

As the company explained during their Q3 earnings call:

We believe that with continued innovation and personalization for individual clinical needs, these benefits will continue to compound across specialties in the future. Monthly online revenue per average subscriber increased 24% in the third quarter relative to last year to $67. A continued shift to more premium personalized offerings has been one contributor towards this dynamic, which has offset headwinds from a migration toward longer duration commitments that carry a lower average monthly price.

The success of our weight-loss specialty has been another key driver in the increase in monthly online revenue per average subscriber through two mechanisms. Firstly, our weight-loss solutions carry a higher monthly average price relative to other product offerings. Historically, our offerings have ranged from $35 to $55 per month depending on specialty and duration.

The end of the shortage is no surprise to Hims. They plan to launch a generic version of Novo Nordisk’s Saxenda (liraglutide), another GLP-1 receptor agonist, later this year. However, liraglutide’s efficacy in weight reduction falls significantly short of semaglutide.

In this randomized clinical trial that included 338 participants, mean body weight change from baseline to 68 weeks was –15.8% with semaglutide vs –6.4% with liraglutide, a statistically significant difference.

As a result, Novo Nordisk has longed deprioritized Saxenda in favor of Wegovy.

Let’s not forget about Eli Lilly’s (LLY) tirzepatide (a GLP-1 and GIP receptor agonist), or Zepbound. The tirzepatide shortage ended in October. Prior to this, Hims offered compounded tirzepatide. In a recent study, tirzepatide proved superior to semaglutide in weight loss, delivering an average weight loss of 20.2%. So liraglutide is quite inferior to both semaglutide and tirzepatide. Hims could differentiate itself by offering liraglutide for a much lower price and as an oral (both tirzepatide and semaglutide are available only as injectables in obesity). Regardless, the end of the shortage for the popular weight loss drugs has a major impact on Hims’ business prospects. In Q3, Hims’ Y/Y subscriber growth was 44%. However, the company did note that “there was 40-plus percent year-over-year growth in our subscribers excluding the GLP-1 business.” The largest impact from the GLP-1 shortage resolution will likely surround Hims’ “monthly online revenue per average subscriber,” which grew 24% Y/Y from $54 in Q3 ’23 to $67 in Q3 ’24. As Hims likes to point out, its weight loss services are “sticky,” with “70% of patients continuing their subscription by 12 weeks.” So, the loss of a lot of their current GLP-1 clientele, coupled with a complete absence of semaglutide/tirzepatide compounds, should become apparent when Hims reports Q2 ’25 earnings later this year. While liraglutide is a sensible fallback, it is not likely to come close to replacing semaglutide and tirzepatide. Hims' providers could still prescribe Wegovy/Zepbound injectables. However, its margins, as a result of not providing the compounded versions themselves, will not be as robust and much of the value proposition associated with a Hims' weight loss subscription will be "lost in translation."

While these developments are a bummer for their weight loss services, all hope is not lost. Hims is riding tailwinds in the global telemedicine market, which is expected to grow at a CAGR of 19% and reach “$432.31 billion by 2032.” They have built a brand, particularly in sensitive areas like sexual health and hair loss, that is compelling and long-lasting.

Right now, I am most interested in whether a quarter slash in Hims’ valuation is justified. To do this, I “reverse-engineered” a discounted cash flow (DCF) analysis (see my bio for model assumptions) to uncover the market’s implicit expectations of growth before and after last week’s valuation adjustment.

Let’s first look at Hims’ financial performance in the trailing twelve months (TTM).

Here are some expectations for 2025:

The Free Cash Flow and FCF margin are implied by Hims’ current market capitalization, projected revenue for 2025, and its P/FCF.

Now, the fun part: If I assume their FCF margin will remain ~13% during the next 9 years, I can adjust the projected Y/Y revenue growth until the model outputs an enterprise value (discounted cash flows + terminal value) that is equal to Hims’ current valuation. In doing so, I can speculate on the market’s implicit expectations for revenue growth.

If we return Hims’ EV to what it was before last week’s 25% haircut, expectations for Y/Y revenue growth were roughly 23.5%. So, the market appears to have lowered its expectations of Y/Y revenue growth by 4.5%. It doesn’t sound like much, but because of growth's compounding nature, slight adjustments can have large repercussions on valuation. Considering everything discussed above, I think this is a reasonable adjustment by market participants.

Financial Health

In Q3, Hims reported revenues of $401.6 million, representing 77% Y/Y growth. Cost of revenues was $83.7 million, or 20.84% of revenues. SG&A and R&D costs totaled $274.4 million and $21.1 million, respectively. Impressively, Hims’ SG&A costs only made up 68% of revenues in Q3. This figure was consistently above 80% just a few quarters back. Net income for Q3 was $75.6 million. Cash from operations was $85.3 million.

After deducting $3.3 in capital expenditure, Hims’ free cash flow was $82 million.

Turning to the balance sheet, as of September 2024, Hims had $165.5 million in cash and equivalents. Short-term investments totaled $88.6 million. Total current assets were $326.4 million, while total current liabilities were $152.6 million. As a result, Hims’ current ratio is roughly 2, which indicates good liquidity. The company is without long-term debt.

Hims will report Q4 earnings on Monday after the market’s close. The revenue estimate is $470.5 million. EPS GAAP is expected to come in at $0.11. Outside of the obvious (e.g., revenue, profitability, subscriber numbers), investors should be focused on management’s insights on how the shortage resolution will impact revenue streams and what strategies are in place to address it, such as the timeline for liraglutide’s rollout. I will be particularly interested in the company’s plan to attract and retain weight loss subscribers in the coming absence of popular GLP-1 compounds.

HIMS Stock: Balanced Take & Investment Recommendation

In conclusion, the GLP-1 shortage resolution is expected to have a material impact on Hims’ growth prospects. As this was not a surprise (both Novo Nordisk and Eli Lilly have invested a lot of money into manufacturing capabilities), it is back to the basics for the company ("core"), including the addition of a substandard GLP-1 to an equation absent of heavy hitters. With or without the popular GLP-1 drugs, Hims is priced for considerable growth (that is now, coincidentally, even with its industry's projected CAGR per my DCF model), and it is likely achievable given its brand and the opportunity that is right in front of Hims. My DCF analysis reveals that Hims is reasonably priced, warranting a “hold” and "market perform" recommendation. Investors should be aware of some idiosyncratic risks, including, but not limited to, a larger than expected impact on Hims’ revenue from the GLP-1 fallout, intense competition from other telemedicine providers like Ro and Amazon’s healthcare unit, tighter controls on prescription-based digital healthcare, rising customer acquisition cost, marketing dependence, high valuation, and execution risk.

data.title: Ally Financial: Planned Credit Card Business Sale Shows Renewed Focus On Core Business
data.description:
The past few years have been a golden time for credit card companies. We've seen new high after new high for companies like Visa (V) and Discover (DFS). Meanwhile, one of the smaller players in that industry, Ally Financial (NYSE:ALLY), just last month announced their intention to sell their credit card business to CardWorks.

As you can see, Ally is still a fair distance from the 52-week highs other credit card firms enjoyed, though they are a few months removed from their 52-week lows as well. I thought it would be interesting to take a closer look at Ally, their recent struggles, and why they decided to get out of the credit card business at a time when others are doing so well in it.

The answer is that Ally intends to focus more on its core businesses, which centers on car loans, but also what amounts to America's largest all-digital bank. Even once they finish selling off the credit cards, Ally has quite a few different side businesses in their portfolio, and the hope is that this renewed focus on their core operations will allow them to recover from what has been a struggle in the inflationary environment of the last couple of years.

Trading Below Book

People continue to tell me that I focus too much on balance sheets in judging stocks. I'm a math guy, I can't help it, and in the case of a financial company like Ally, I feel like the balance sheet gives us some very good insights into what this diverse company looks like at present.

Cash and Equivalents

$13.9 billion

(source: most recent 10-K from SEC)

Whether it's deposits from customers, credit card loans, or car loans, much of Ally's business is centered around credit. Even once they divest of the credit card business, that basic fact isn't going to change. The debt/equity ratio of 12.80 does suggest they're rather highly leveraged at present, though their long-term debt/equity ratio is fairly modest.

The price/book ratio, which of course isn't the end-all, be-all for valuation, is also quite low, 0.84 at present prices. The financial sector leans toward low price/book ratios at present, but Ally is even lower still, with the sector median 1.30.

A discount to book always attracts my attention, personally, even though there generally are reasons for it. Ally's low price/book ratio, in my opinion, is in no small part a function of a market sentiment that is the result of earnings struggles in the last two years.

A Weak 2024 for Earnings, But a Cash Flow Silver Lining

For pure credit card plays, the high interest rates and inflationary environment led to boom times. For Ally, with its deeper connection to vehicle loans, it's a different story. Car loans financed through deposits work differently as a business model than general credit card debt companies, seeing less near-term boost from higher interest rates. Expenses, however, still grow as those rates rise.

Ally has been progressively growing their business, but the interest rate environment just wasn't conducive to that, leading to a better bottom line. In 2024, Ally sourced another $39 billion in loans from 14.6 million new car financing applications. That's a record for them, and speaks to the business continuing apace, even if it didn't translate to as much net income.

There are encouraging signs about the strength of those new applicants, as 44% of them were from the highest credit tier. They should be relatively secure debt, and hopefully that will mean the provisions for losses won't need to be so high in the future.

The corporate finance and banking business is also doing well in adding more depositors, adding 230,000 to that franchise in 2024. According to their conference call, nearly 40% of those depositors are what they consider "engaged savers." They view engaged savers as less sensitive to interest rates in continuing to add to their deposits, which hopefully will mean as interest rates start to come down, they'll continue to add money on that side of things, allowing Ally to issue even more loans.

On a GAAP basis, the P/E ratio is 20.97, above the sector median of 13.29. The non-GAAP P/E is 16.06, which is also a bit above the sector median of 12.30. I view both of these cases as reflective of the interest rate environment in 2024, which was great for some parts of the financial sector, but not so great for others. From a cash flow perspective, though, 2024 wasn't nearly so grim.

(source: most recent 10-K from SEC)

Despite EPS dropping noticeably from 2023 to 2024, operating FCF was virtually flat. Ally also had better investing FCF because of gains on sales of investments, and they ended up improving their cash position in what appears likely to be their worst year in this economic cycle.

Signs of Recovery

While they have been worse than the sector with respect to trailing P/E ratio, the estimates going forward tell a different story for the future. Estimates are that in 2025, their net revenue will be $8.56 billion and non-GAAP EPS of $3.61. That would be a Forward P/E of 10.46, putting them roughly in line with the sector median, which is 11.42.

2026 looks even better, with revenue for Ally growing to $9.32 billion, and EPS back up to $5.53. If those estimates pan out, that should augur quite well for stock prices, and puts Ally in more of a value position, assuming the rebound in near-future earnings.

Selling Off the Credit Card Business

You may think that the estimates, which haven't broadly changed in the last month, don't reflect Ally's prospects with the impending sale of the credit card business. Yet in the most recent conference call, Ally reported that they believe the sale will not have any material impact on pretax income going forward.

Instead, the sale is expected to add about $1 per share to tangible book value of Ally's stock, bringing that already low price/book ratio even lower. Doing that without impacting income is a pretty encouraging move.

Ally Credit had 1.3 million active credit card users, and $2.3 billion in credit card receivables, which you'll notice is a paltry sum compared to their overall receivables on the balance sheet. It was very much a side business for Ally. The sale is expected to complete some time in 2025, and given its small size it almost certainly won't face regulatory challenges.

That's not to say it will have no impact at all on future operations statements. In the conference call, they conceded that the removal of the credit card business would mean somewhat lower gross margins in the near-term which, again, is the reality of the interest rate environment right now. On the other hand, eliminating the business will lower their operating expenses, which is why at the end of the day, they expect it to be more or less pretax income neutral.

Given how lucrative credit card businesses can be these days, there is certainly an argument for Ally to get deeper into it, not divest from it entirely. At the same time, adding to their book value makes the case that they're getting a good price out of it.

Dividends and Repurchases

Ally pays a 30¢ quarterly dividend, and that's very sustainable. On the other hand, it represents a yield of 3.18%, which is scarcely above the sector median of 2.93%. Though it's not a bad yield, this hardly makes Ally an exciting income stock in and of itself.

What might be more enticing is the prospect of share repurchases. In the context of discussing the credit card business sale, Ally emphasized their history of returning capital to investors through share repurchases. Given the stock is trading below its underlying book value, that could be a very appealing option, and if market sentiment puts share prices more in-line with sector median price/book ratios, it could be a very effective way to return value to shareholders.

Renewed Focus Still Carries Risk

The estimates of earnings growth in the next few years tell a story where Ally recovers strongly, and the company sees that happening with a renewed focus on growing deposits and expanding their car dealer financial relationships.

But reducing their diversity and focusing on their largest business segments carries some amount of risk. The hope is that with interest rates starting to come down, the car loan business with improve, and focusing on that aims to maximize the efficiency of that particular segment. But that's a best-case scenario.

No one has a crystal ball about where interest rates are ultimately going, let alone the future of the car market. If inflation starts to pick up more, the expected drops in interest rates may be slowed, and if the economy weakens, the car market may not be nearly so appealing a business to be in.

A lot of that business also depends on the GM and Stellantis dealerships they work with and their customers. If those companies don't fare as well as other automakers' dealerships, Ally could see less interest in new loans.

Finally, while Ally is emphasizing their new loans as involving a lot of applicants in the highest credit tier, there is always a risk of default. While better credit ratings from applicants should mean fewer defaults, economic woes can mean problems for unexpected people.

Questions Remain, But Ally Is Reasonably Priced

Ally is no longer even tangentially a credit card business, but they are a very large car loan-centric company. If you believe in the future prospects of that industry, it seems like Ally's current share price is very modest. A forward P/E of just 10.46 is not bad, and the prospect of share repurchases could lead to current prices looking cheap down the road.

I'm rating it a buy, albeit not a strong buy because I question how quickly the interest rates are going to come down, and how long it will take the market to recognize the potential here once the earnings start to improve.

Investors in Ally should keep a close eye on the auto business because now more than ever, that segment is their bread and butter. It is also worth keeping an eye on Ally's ability to attract deposits because, after all, that's where they're getting the capital to make those car loans. Strong profitability is going to depend on both of those aspects.

The credit card business may be exciting right now, but the car loan business has potential to make quite a bit of money, and Ally has a history of being a strong player there.

data.title: IonQ Q4 Preview: Microsoft Quantum Game Changer
data.description:
Co-Authored by Noah Cox and Brock Heilig

Investment Thesis

I believe IonQ (NYSE:IONQ) is a promising quantum networking company that is still in the R&D (Research and Development) stage with the USAF (United States Air Force). I believe the DOD will be one of the biggest beneficiaries of quantum computing to help build an edge on the battlefield. The USAF is investing millions in accelerating quantum development. They know the stakes are high.

With this, I think earnings this upcoming week are potentially a game changer for quantum, with Microsoft announcing their key new quantum chip. I believe this new chip architecture will change the game for quantum computing. It is accelerating the timeline that IonQ will be able to get out of the research and development phase and into a commercialization phase.

With this, I believe shares are a strong buy going into Q4 earnings.

Background

I’ve never written on IonQ before, so I think it’s important to do a deep dive into the background of the company and what IonQ actually does.

According to Amazon AWS (an IonQ partner):

IonQ is a leader in quantum computing, offering some of the most useful and capable quantum computers in the industry.

The company was launched in 2015 by co-founder Chris Monroe in College Park, Maryland. Monroe launched the company 10 years ago after completing decades of research.

Pentagram writes:

...[IonQ is] developing the world’s most advanced quantum computer. Using a new approach called ion trapping, IonQ’s quantum cores point lasers at single-atom qubits to perform longer, more sophisticated calculations with fewer errors than any quantum computer yet built.

IonQ is unique in that they are (I believe) the best quantum focused company with a real path to commercialization. While Microsoft (MSFT) and Google (GOOGL) have both come out with Quantum chips over the past 3 months, IonQ is focused on actionable research to directly apply their IP. That’s why their acquisition of Qubitekk is key for me.

At the beginning of 2025, IonQ completed their acquisition of Qubitekk via all of its assets. Qubitekk is a “prominent quantum networking company,” according to IonQ’s press release, and the acquisition will reportedly help establish IonQ as one of the main leaders in quantum computing.

“The acquisition of Qubitekk is a key step in IonQ’s journey to lead quantum networking into the future,” said Jordan Shapiro, who is the Head of Corporate Development and VP of Financial Planning & Analysis at IonQ.

Qubitekk’s innovative work in quantum networking aligns perfectly with our vision of creating scalable quantum systems that transform industries. By combining our strengths, we are not just expanding IonQ’s capabilities in critical areas like defense and cybersecurity, we expect to accelerate the development of quantum networks, the backbone of tomorrow’s quantum internet -Shapiro.

Interestingly, IonQ has signed two contracts with the United States Air Force Research Lab. (AFRL) in the last six months. The first contract was signed back in September and was worth a total of $54.5 million. The second contract was signed in January, and that contract was worth $21.1 million. This second contract came from their acquisition of Qubitekk.

Governments, businesses, and academic institutions are using quantum computing to focus on solving problems in areas like healthcare, finance, and chemistry. IonQ President and CEO Peter Chapman described the first contract as an inflection point.

We are thrilled to be at a major inflection point for the company and the industry, Chapman said.

No other publicly traded pure-play quantum computing company has nearly doubled revenue each year since going public, nor have they approached the $100M bookings mark so quickly.

While the first contract had more of a generalized goal, the second contract signed in January was much more direct. The purpose of the latest contract was to “install quantum network infrastructure at the Innovare Advancement Center in Rome, New York.”

Why Microsoft’s Chip Changes The Game

For quantum, I think we saw key news emerged from Redmond, Washington on Wednesday when Microsoft announced the creation of its own quantum computing chip called Majorana 1 (this comes on the heels of Google’s quantum chip, Willow). According to CNBC, Microsoft was finally able to release its first quantum computing chip after nearly two decades of research in the field.

While a competitor coming out with its own quantum computing chip seems like a bad thing for IonQ, this is actually good for the company because it shows that this type of technology is not “decades away,” as some from Nvidia have claimed.

This means that the future of the quantum computing industry is close. It means the future is bright. Many of these companies saw their stocks jump coming off the news of Microsoft’s new chip. According to Yahoo Finance, IonQ was up 2.1%, Rigetti Computing rose 6.9% and D-Wave Quantum saw an 11.1% jump.

This Microsoft quantum chip is incredibly powerful. According to Tech Informed, Microsoft argues that Majorana 1 will be able to solve complex industrial and societal challenges. Additionally, the chip could have possibilities of scaling upwards of 1 million qubits.

Right now, the Majorana 1 has just eight qubits, according to CNBC, but Microsoft’s Chetan Nayak claims that the company has “worked out a path to a million.” This is ahead of Google’s Willow chip.

And interestingly, after taking nearly two decades to develop the first quantum computing chip with just eight qubits as of right now, Microsoft CEO Satya Nadella said on a recent podcast that the path to 1 million qubits is not that far off. Now that the company has developed eight qubits, things should start moving faster.

When asked by podcast host Dwarkesh Patel what the timeline looks like to scale up to 1 million qubits, Nadella said it should be sometime between 2027-29.

Maybe '27, '28, '29, we will be able to actually build this, Nadella said. Now that we have this one gate, can I put the thing into an integrated circuit and then actually put these integrated circuits into a real computer? That is where the next logical step is.

What This Means And Q4 Preview

For me, I think this means the focus on quantum is going to now realistically shift to applications and commercialization (from R&D). What this means for IonQ is that quantum demand should accelerate. Again, now that expectations have shifted and this isn’t “decades away” like some thought, this should seriously expedite IonQ’s timeline. I believe the company has a strong backdrop for earnings heading into Wednesday. Earnings-per-share estimates show losses, while the revenue estimates are very bullish.

On the earnings-per-share front, IonQ is projected to post an earnings-per-share of -$0.25, although this prediction is a combination of just two analysts’ predictions. If IonQ meets this number, this would represent -25.00% year-over-year growth. To me, these losses (and the fact they grew) are acceptable. The company is scaling.

On the revenue front, four analysts estimate that IonQ will bring in $10.28 million in Q4. If the company meets this combined estimate, it would represent 68.38% year-over-year growth.

Over the last three months, there hasn’t been a lot of action in terms of revisions, but the small amounts of action there have been has been positive. Both the earnings-per-share and revenue revisions have seen just one revision over the past three months, and both of them have been upward. Even though there isn’t a great sample size to go off of, this shows that analysts are becoming more optimistic about IonQ heading into Q4 earnings with such a small group tracking the company.

In the last few weeks, IonQ signed a key partnership agreement with General Dynamics in an effort to “drive innovation and accelerate quantum computing capabilities for government missions.” These solutions should help the US government (and other key General Dynamics customers) build more quantum networks that are resistant to hacking.

Valuation

While IonQ shares are expensive on a traditional valuation method on a forward basis I think this makes sense. The company does not currently have a forward price-to-earnings ratio (because they lose money). But we can look at their forward price-to-book ratio and see that it is at a premium to the sector median.

When we look at the forward price-to-book ratio, it is currently at 16.79, which is a nearly 300% premium to the sector median of just 4.22. Seeking Alpha Quant gives IonQ a grade of a D- on this metric.

But when we look at IonQ’s forward revenue growth, we can see that this is also at a major premium to the sector median. IonQ has projected forward revenue growth of 95.51%. This is a strong 1,397.03% premium to the sector median, which is just 6.38%. Seeking Alpha Quant gives IonQ a grade of an A+ on this metric.

This is clearly a much higher premium than the price-to-book premium, which is just shy of 300%.

I think IonQ is a case of GARP (Growth at a reasonable price). While, yes, shares are expensive on a price to book basis, top line growth should in the long run grow fast enough to help bring valuation metrics down, while also allowing investors to still pick up a strong return.

In my opinion, I think shares should trade at a 400% price-to-book premium above the sector median. If we saw a 400% premium on this metric, this would mean approximately a 33% upside for IonQ shares.

Risks

Quantum is starting to accelerate. But I think the biggest risk for IonQ right now is something that I alluded to earlier. These quantum computing chips (both from Microsoft and Google) are still essentially brand-new developments. The market is still largely a blue ocean, and there will be a lot of tech companies that will be looking to get a foot in the door and earn market share in this industry. Most of IonQ’s valuation is built on the assumption they will be able to carve a key spot in the quantum sector over the next 10 years. The stock is almost entirely based on forward growth. Any deviation from the assumption that they’ll be an industry leader could really hurt shares.

As I mentioned earlier, three quantum computing companies saw their stocks rise coming off the news that Microsoft developed its own Majorana 1 chip. IonQ is arguably the public market leader for pure quantum and has paying revenue contracts now for quantum research. So for them, news that quantum chips with a commercial application could only be a couple years away is not as earth-shattering. They’ve already found a way to monetize quantum. Rigetti Computing (RGTI) and D-Wave Quantum (QBTS) have far less revenue.

This is (I believe) IonQ’s advantage. With the two USAF recent research contracts, I believe that they are well ahead of any other potential competitors because they have a customer that is paying them for research today and product tomorrow. While Microsoft and Google are building chips, IonQ is building solutions.

Bottom Line

While IonQ’s earnings are a quarterly ritual, I think this time feels different for the entire quantum computing space. The entire quantum computing industry has been jolted (in a good way) by these key developments at Microsoft and Google, which show that the world of Quantum is coming a lot faster than what investors and scientists had originally predicted. Based on the two research contracts that IonQ has signed with USAF, I think that they are in a great position to capitalize on some of these developments. While shares are risky from a traditional valuation standpoint, I think there is interesting upside left for the equity for investors who want to take a long-term view on shares.

The development that Microsoft has had with its chip, Majorana 1, has completely reshaped and expedited the timeline for quantum computing, bringing this conversation from a decades away philosophy to a years away reality. Microsoft has proved true quantum is close, and I think IonQ is in a great position to take advantage of this.

With this, I think shares are a strong buy heading into the quarterly report.

data.title: Going Brown: Shorting DSI To Bet Against ESG
data.description:
Editor's note: Seeking Alpha is proud to welcome Phaseshift Research as a new contributing analyst. You can become one too! Share your best investment idea by submitting your article for review to our editors. Get published, earn money, and unlock exclusive SA Premium access. Click here to find out more »

Introduction

The new U.S. Administration has assumed its functions only a few weeks ago, notwithstanding this, some important, new policy trends are apparent in budgetary matters and in the real economy. One segment of the economy affected by these policy shifts is Environment, Social, Governance [ESG]. In this context, we may refer to the already-taken decisions of pulling out of the Paris Climate Agreement, slashing USAID spending and further deregulation and defunding in line with the above. These measures, along with others that are already in the pipeline, will inevitably impact ESG-based equity valuations. While having no judgment whatsoever regarding the policy shifts in Washington, it is clear that a possibility is opening to capitalize on the idea of shorting the iShares ESG MSCI KLD 400 ETF (NYSEARCA:DSI) and longing the SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY) to isolate and bet against the ESG factor.

Thesis

There is a clear and escalating pattern of ESG-abandonment, and there are serious reasons to believe that this trend will at least persist, if not strengthen, over the next four years.

It appears that there is broad GOP support for the decisions of the new Administration to roll-back environmental regulation as well as Diversity, Equality and Inclusion [DEI] programs. On 28 January 2025 Republican-Party leaning officials (auditors, comptrollers and treasurers) from 18 states co-signed a letter to the Securities and Exchange Commission [SEC] urging for the prohibition of ESG asset management mandates. The new Department of Government Efficiency [DOGE], and its first steps in the direction of institutional ESG defunding and regulation, is a sign that points towards the long-term nature of the shift, rather than a short-term campaign. Symptomatically, major private-sector players have been following down a similar path. Since 2023, BlackRock CEO, Larry Fink, once a vehement ESG proponent, has conspicuously dropped the term entirely. In 2024, J.P. Morgan, along with Goldman Sachs and several other major asset managers, left the Climate Action 100+ alliance. Most recently, in December 2024, the Fifth Circuit Court of Appeals ruled to drop Nasdaq’s board diversity rules.

Strategy

To capitalize on the above-described policy shift, it is possible to isolate and bet against the ESG factor. To do this, we can short DSI and hedge U.S. market exposure by hedging with a SPY long, re-balancing the hedge on a weekly basis.

DSI is the ticker for the iShares MSCI KLD 400 Social ETF - it seeks to track an index composed of companies that have positive ESG characteristics, drawn from a universe of the 3,000 largest U.S. public equities. SPY is the ticker for the SPDR S&P 500 ETF - and it is a market-cap weighted composition of the 500 largest U.S. public equities.

To attempt to isolate the ESG factor (returns attributable to changes in ESG) we can take a Capital Asset Pricing Model [CAPM]-like approach.

We define SPY as the CAPM market portfolio,

We define DSI as a regular CAPM security with the addition of an idiosyncratic ESG premium,

We define the strategy as an outright short on DSI and a SPY long, levered by DSI’s beta,

Substituting the given values into the equation and simplifying, we obtain:

Which for simplicity can be approximated as:

It should be noted that in the derivation above, we add ESG as an alpha as opposed to a factor; this is because ESG tends to behave as more of a discount/premium than as a true factor. Moreover, in the penultimate step, the first term is discarded, this will result in a very subtle misestimation of the strategy’s returns.

From an implementation standpoint, we start by shorting DSI and longing SPY levered to DSI’s beta, however, as time elapses and the securities’ prices diverge, the hedge will lose its accuracy. For this reason, we must rebalance the strategy on a regular basis (weekly rebalancing represents a reasonable trade-off between the discrete hedging error and transaction fees). Rebalancing simply implies making sure that our SPY size = - DSI size * DSI beta.

Shown above are the cumulative daily returns of DSI, SPY and the aforementioned ESG-short strategy over the last 190 days. The strategy is 3x levered to more closely match the standard deviation of SPY. The historical performance of the strategy is mixed; however, the post-inauguration performance (+7.73%) immediately tells us that we are not the only ones looking to short ESG. Whether 7.73% (2.58% unlevered) is accurately representative of the ongoing structural shifts in ESG investing, is subject to many questions. The impact of longitudinal policy initiatives on ESG are difficult to accurately predict, and in such circumstances allocators frequently act on a more conservative basis. It is reasonable to believe that much of this Administration’s ESG reform is yet to be priced accurately, and is likely being under-priced. Providing price targets is unreasonable as this idea is highly event-driven and presents very high potential magnitudinal variation.

Above, we have the strategy’s daily return distribution along with some distributional characteristics for SPY, DSI and the strategy itself [ESG]. Although, as the age-old adage goes “past returns do not guarantee future results” we can look at the return distribution to try and gauge the long-term behavior of the strategy. The strategy distribution is slightly favorable with a positive skew (large positive returns occur more frequently than large negative returns). Whether a Sharpe ratio of 7.56% can be seen as positive or not is dependent on the investor’s opportunity set and conviction in the trade-idea. A kurtosis (tail thickness) of 2.4 is fine, however, given the positive skew a higher kurtosis would be more favorable. Looking at the post-inauguration metrics, the strategy seems very attractive - although it is unlikely for those statistics to persist for the next four years. All-in-all we have a comfortable distributional setup that allows us to focus on the trade-idea without having to excessively worry about what will happen to the position if the catalysts fall-through.

On the more practical side of the strategy, there are a few considerations, starting with the holding period. Given the fact that this trade-idea leans on the intentions of the current Administration, a four-year holding period is well-grounded. This is not to be confused with the expected holding period, which is highly contingent on the exit-conditions. The key trigger that will result in an exit of the trade is a significant loss of political anti-ESG momentum. Once there are no more new initiatives and less direct pressure, even if the political sentiment around ESG continues to be negative - that sentiment will become the new status quo and this trade idea will be fully priced by the market. The other exit-condition is a change in the course taken by the U.S. Administration regarding ESG, while this is unlikely, it should still be considered.

In order to execute the short leg of the strategy, DSI must be borrowed. Borrowing rates on DSI are typically near-zero for institutional investors and ~0.3% per annum for retail investors (of course, this may differ from broker to broker). SPY’s trailing twelve-month average daily volume is ~53m units and DSI’s is ~110k units. Therefore, liquidity issues are unlikely.

With regard to alternative strategies that create similar exposure we can look to options. One possibility is a relative value spread to isolate the ESG factor (buy DSI puts at the money, buy SPY calls at the money with quantity scaled to match DSI’s beta). If executed correctly this may work even better than the strategy on margin, however, there is one major drawback - time decay. As aforementioned the expected holding period of the strategy is uncertain and there is also doubt as to when the catalysts will materialize. This is a big problem in options, as we have to choose an expiration - too short and the catalysts won’t materialize, too long and our returns will be heavily damped. While there are multi-leg strategies and expiration-selection approaches that may mitigate time risk, they are beyond the scope of this article.

Catalysts & Transmission

There are two key catalysts that will contribute to the withering of ESG:

1) The direct impact of ESG deregulation and de-funding

2) The indirect social impact of an Administration that opts not to pursue ESG-conscious initiatives

Transmission Mechanism:

The direct impact of ESG deregulation and defunding is primarily on the supply side. Companies are currently subject to myriads of ESG regulations and incentive-mechanisms and, if the U.S. Administration follows-through on what it is signaling, there is a long way down.

ESG deregulation and de-funding -> Corporate incentives decrease -> ESG project IRR decreases -> ESG projects provide lower returns -> ESG factor decreases.

Rationale: This mechanism is straight-forward. If corporations are not required or financially remunerated to do something, they have little reason to act. In the same vein, if corporate ESG requirements are laxed and government cheques are thinned, ESG projects will yield lower returns. According to Rob Du Boff at Bloomberg Intelligence: "Deregulation is one lever Republicans can use to kill ESG". Furthermore, allocators and corporations may find aligning themselves with the current administration's sentiment opportunistic; further raising the opportunity cost of pursuing ESG projects.

Transmission Mechanism:

The potential indirect demand-side impact of ESG deregulation and de-funding is distinctive.

ESG deregulation and de-funding -> Social relevance decreases -> Demand decreases -> ESG factor decreases.

Rationale: ESG investments are a highly demand-driven market, and whether ESG integration in financial analysis produces outsized returns is still subject to much speculation. In the words of Dr. John Seo, Co-Founder of Fermat Capital Management: "They're basically taking the worst fears of a hundred years of climate change, upper bound, and then pricing it in as the here and now". Governments' views trickle-down on their nations' constituents. If the U.S. government does not care - Americans will eventually start caring less, and given the demand-driven nature of ESG assets, their prices will naturally decrease.

Risks & Counters

Politically, much uncertainty surrounds the future. There is, however, a geopolitical counterargument to be made against the U.S. veering away from ESG investment - China. China is a behemoth in green technology - from electric vehicles to wind turbines to solar. The U.S. entirely pulling out of ESG essentially defaults their market share of these industries to its biggest competitor. This is a dubious and perhaps a risky move. Therefore, it is unreasonable to expect a full ESG ban or similar.

Strategically, there are several important considerations. We isolate the ESG factor by shorting DSI and longing SPY. This prompts two primary assumptions: SPY represents the true market portfolio; DSI's unique variance accurately and exclusively represents the theoretical ESG factor. Fortunately, it is unlikely that these assumptions are violated too severely, to where the strategy's viability is put into question.

Since we need to hedge-out U.S. market equity exposure and constant re-hedging is impossible, we risk incorrectly tracking the ESG factor as the ETFs' prices change. Furthermore, the ETFs themselves may not accurately weight the constituent securities. The use of leverage to match SPY's return volatility is not necessary, however, in the delineated example it poses various risks, such as margin risk and amplified losses. The short position on DSI also poses the risk of an unbounded downside.

Transmission mechanism-wise, it is important to consider that U.S. corporate equity is not solely held by U.S. based investors and approximately 17% is held internationally. While the rest of the world is also impacted by U.S. policy, allocators are ultimately still driven by their own nations' governments, which may still provide some incentives to remain ESG-aligned. Moreover, the link between deregulation/de-funding and social relevance is extremely complex, and the strength and lags of that transmission are certainly up for debate. Ultimately, everything will depend on how aggressively the current Administration will choose to act.

Notwithstanding some uncertainties and marginal risks, the radical changes in policy being instituted by the new U.S. Administration clearly represent opportunities for alpha generation.

data.title: Exact Sciences: A Strong Q4 Reveals Signs Of A Potential Inflection Point
data.description:
It has been over a year since my last Exact Sciences (NASDAQ:EXAS) article from August 2023, when I highlighted the company's encouraging Q2 results, including a 24% year-over-year growth in core revenue and a 31% bump in screening revenue. In addition, Exact upgraded their full-year guidance, however, the market responded negatively, dropping the share price from around $96 to about $82 per share. Although I was a bit apprehensive about their ability to sustain that growth trajectory, I still had a bullish outlook for the company due to the fundamentals and a strong performance from their flagship product, Cologuard. Since then, I have watched EXAS continue to struggle to arrange a strong reversal setup below my Buy Threshold of $62 per share. As a result, the ticker has been gathering dust in my Compounding Healthcare “Bio Boom” speculative portfolio. Admittedly, I have been content with leaving my EXAS in a “House Money” condition as the new administration begins to implement their own policies that could have a dramatic impact on the healthcare sector’s near-term and long-term outlook.

However, I had been looking forward to the company’s Q4 earnings report to see if they were able to meet the Street’s projections and any updates on their 2025 guidance. Well, Exact posted mixed earnings with a solid beat on revenue, but a big miss on EPS. The market did not react to the headline numbers positively, with the share price going from roughly $50 per share down to $46 per share in the subsequent trading session. Indeed, the big miss on EPS did cause some whiplash, a deeper dive revealed that the company has continued to make progress in their core business, and they are expecting strong growth in 2025. Therefore, I am going to keep an eye on EXAS in the coming days and weeks to see if the ticker can finally provide a quality reversal setup that will entice me to take the position out of mothballs.

I intend to review the company’s Q4 earnings and will provide my views on the quarter. In addition, I will take a look at Exact’s 2025 guidance and see how that stacks up to the Street’s expectations. In addition, I point out a few notable downside risks that investors should consider when managing their EXAS position. Finally, I will attempt to figure out if EXAS is worthy of a buy at these valuations and will try to identify any opportunities for me to add to my dormant EXAS position.

Q4 Review

Taking a look at Exact’s Q4 earnings report, the company’s total revenue $713M, which was up 10% year-over-year and up 11% year-over-year on a core basis. The company screening revenue was up 14% to $553M and their Precision Oncology revenue was up slightly to $161M. The company’s adjusted EBITDA of $75M, up $26M over Q4 of 2023. The company also reported their gross margin was 69% with adjusted coming in at 72%. Looking at the cash flow, the company reported that their operating cash flow $47M, with their free cash flow hitting $11M.

Turning to the sticker shock item… the company reported a net loss of $865M, which led to -$4.67 per share, whereas they reported a $49.8M net loss (-$0.27 per share) in Q4 of last year. This was due to $838M impairment charges related to the Thrive acquisition.

In terms of their cash position, the company left 2024 with $1.04B cash, equivalents, and marketable securities.

In addition to the financials, Exact also discussed some important updates, including that they have secured Medicare pricing for Cologuard Plus, which is set to launch with coverage and guideline inclusion next quarter. Exact is also planning on launching Oncodetect in Q2 after a reimbursement submission, along with Cancerguard EX in the second half of this year.

My Take On The Quarter

I did not need to dig very deep to realize that the market's negative reaction to Exact’s Q4 earnings was overblown. Admittedly, the headline EPS figure was shocking, but this was largely due to an $838M impairment charge from the Thrive acquisition. I have to imagine this was a pending issue and might have been factored into the share price at one point in time. In addition, this impairment charge appears to be a one-time event tied to specific circumstances rather than an outcome of the company’s performance.

The company's 10.2% year-over-year revenue growth did exceed expectations, and strong demand for the Cologuard brand suggests their core operations are gaining momentum. Furthermore, the adjusted EBITDA of $75M, along with an improved margin of 11%, suggests the company is growing efficiency and making progress towards moving to profitability.

In addition, the company’s strong cash position of $1.04B and positive cash flow figures should provide some assurance the company's financials are strong enough to allow them to maintain their growth initiatives and keep their pipeline moving. The company expects to launch a few new products this year, so having the finances and established commercial infrastructure should allow those programs to launch smoothly and help accelerate growth in the coming years.

Again, I understand why the market is going to fixate on the negative headline numbers, but the remaining components of the report suggest that Exact is ready for growth and will be able to defend their position as a leading brand in the cancer diagnostics market.

Reviewing the 2025 Guidance

Although 2024 wasn’t a strong year for Exact Sciences, the company is confident about this year and has set ambitious revenue guidance for 2025, with total revenues between $3.025B and $3.085B, which would be up 11% year-over-year. The company anticipates the upcoming launch of Cologuard Plus in Q2 to fuel growth in screening revenue, which is estimated to fall between $2.35B and $2.39B.

In addition, Exact has set their adjusted EBITDA guidance to $410M-$440M, up from $323M in 2024.

Can They Do It?

Well, in addition to some of the pending growth drivers, I would also point to the company’s track record for growth. Exact has reported year-over-year revenue growth for each quarter since Q1 of 2020 often exceeding the Street’s expectations.

Although the earnings per share frequently misses expectations, the general earnings trend suggests the company is heading towards sustained profitability once they get their efficiency and costs under control.

The Street does expect Exact to continue to report strong revenue growth into the next decade, nearly doubling their revenue by 2034.

More importantly, the Street expects the company to report a positive EPS for 2025 and maintain a positive EPS through 2034.

So, one could argue that the company’s optimistic 2025 guidance is a signal that we are in the midst of a significant inflection point in the Exact’s history.

Risks to Consider

Despite my bullish views, I must point out some risks that investors should consider when managing their positions. First, Exact has struggled to make a strong and durable push toward profitability over the past few years, despite nearly doubling their revenue since 2020. Certainly, I understand that the company needs to spend to grow, and the general trend has been up. Plus, we need to consider the impact of COVID on their base business. However, one must concede the erratic earnings do not translate well to the share price. Despite being the name brand in colon cancer screening, Exact appears to have a setback right as they seem ready to cross the threshold of profitability. The company did miss on both revenue and EPS in Q3, which forced the company to amend their 2024 guidance. I believe investors need to accept that EXAS will continue to struggle because the market might not trust the company’s progress until it is almost undeniable.

In addition, investors need to consider the company’s financial health and their ability to get to profitability. The company had roughly $1.04B at the end of 2024, which should be sufficient to get them through 2025. However, the company has a history of erratic cash usage and has an average quarterly cash burn rate of around $86M since Q2 of 2022. The company’s cash runway would be roughly 3 years at that rate. Again, I wouldn’t say their cash position is an immediate threat. However, the pending launches could change that outlook. An underwhelming launch or lackluster growth could have the Street turning on Exact in the coming quarters as the likelihood the company could require a secondary offering to bridge them to profitability.

Considering these risks, I am maintaining my EXAS conviction level at 3 out of 5 and the ticker will remain in the Compounding Healthcare “Bio Boom” speculative portfolio for the foreseeable future.

My Plan

I am looking to turn the market's initial reaction into an opportunity to grab some shares of EXAS in anticipation the company will begin to make a strong push toward profitability and deliver some long-term value. The company has pending launches in 2025 that could be the fuel to sustain long-term growth into the next decade.

Finding a fair price for EXAS can be challenging, but using the price-to-sales model with Exact’s 2034 projected revenue of $5.94B and the industry’s average price-to-sales ratio of 4x to 5x, we can say that the company could be valued between $23.76B and $29.7B, which be about $128-$160 per share. Indeed, that is what the company could be valued at nearly a decade from now, but a fair value in 2025 can be estimated if we discount that valuation for nine years at a 10%-12% rate, giving us $45 to $68 per share. The ticker is trading towards the lower end of that range, around $46-$50 per share. So, I am eyeing for an opportunity to click the buy button.

Looking at the Daily Chart, we can see that EXAS has been under strong selling pressure after their Q3 earnings report in November, and is now trading below my Buy Level 1 of $52 per share.

The share price bounced off $46 per share, forming a nice hammer candle under elevated trading volume. In addition, we can see a bullish divergence in the RSI. So, I believe there are some ingredients for a potential reversal here. However, the ticker is very bearish on the go-no-go indicator and is trading below two anchored-VWAPs. Plus, the downtrend ray from the November high remains overhead.

So, I am going to wait to see what the market is going to do with EXAS in the coming days and weeks. If the ticker remains at these levels for another week, I will consider making a small addition based on the valuation. Then, I will wait for the ticker to break the downtrend ray and climb above the anchored VWAPs before committing to another buy below my “Buy Threshold” of $62 per share.

On the other hand, if the ticker continues to sell off, I will wait for a retest of the June 2024 lows of around $41 per share to formulate a new strategy.

Once I have executed my buy orders, I will set equivalent sell orders at my Sell Targets in order to book some profits and move my position back to a “House Money” status to hold for a long-term investment.
